d\l<m
Concrete date/time and related types.
See http://www.iana.org/time-zones/repository/tz-link.html for
time zone and DST data sources.

Z!d8d9
Z"d:d;
Z#d<d=
Z$d>S
Represent the difference between two datetime objects.
    Supported operators:
    - add, subtract timedelta
    - unary plus, minus, abs
    - compare to timedelta
    - multiply, divide by int
    In addition, datetime supports subtraction of two datetime objects
    returning a timedelta, and addition or subtraction of a datetime
    and a timedelta giving a datetime.
    Representation: (days, seconds, microseconds).  Why?  Because I
    felt like it.
    )
_days
_seconds
_microseconds
	_hashcoder	
}	n@t
|	d	
;z$timedelta # of days is too large: %dr

Z'dBdC
Concrete date type.
    Constructors:
    __new__()
    fromtimestamp()
    today()
    fromordinal()
    Operators:
    __repr__, __str__
    __eq__, __le__, __lt__, __ge__, __gt__, __hash__
    __add__, __radd__, __sub__ (add/radd only with timedelta arg)
    Methods:
    timetuple()
    toordinal()
    weekday()
    isoweekday(), isocalendar(), isoformat()
    ctime()
    strftime()
    Properties (readonly):
    year, month, day
    )
_year
_month
_dayr
zVConstructor.
        Arguments:
        year, month, day (required, base 1)
        Nr

date.todayc
Construct a date from a proleptic Gregorian ordinal.
        January 1 of year 1 is day 1.  Only the year, month and day are
        non-zero in the result.
        )

z|Construct a date from the ISO year, week number and weekday.
        This is the inverse of the date.isocalendar() functionz

date.fromisocalendarc
Convert to formal string, for repr().
        >>> dt = datetime(2010, 1, 1)
        >>> repr(dt)
        'datetime.datetime(2010, 1, 1, 0, 0)'
        >>> dt = datetime(2010, 1, 1, tzinfo=timezone.utc)
        >>> repr(dt)
        'datetime.datetime(2010, 1, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)'
        z

date.__format__c
Return the date formatted according to ISO.
        This is 'YYYY-MM-DD'.
        References:
        - http://www.w3.org/TR/NOTE-datetime
        - http://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/~mgk25/iso-time.html
        z
%04d-%02d-%02d)

date.timetuplec
Return proleptic Gregorian ordinal for the year, month and day.
        January 1 of year 1 is day 1.  Only the year, month and day values
        contribute to the result.
        )

z.Subtract two dates, or a date and a timedelta.)
days1
days2r
date.__sub__c
z:Return day of the week, where Monday == 0 ... Sunday == 6.r7
date.weekdayc
z:Return day of the week, where Monday == 1 ... Sunday == 7.r8
isoweekdayD
date.isoweekdayc
Return a named tuple containing ISO year, week number, and weekday.
        The first ISO week of the year is the (Mon-Sun) week
        containing the year's first Thursday; everything else derives
        from that.
        The first week is 1; Monday is 1 ... Sunday is 7.
        ISO calendar algorithm taken from
        http://www.phys.uu.nl/~vgent/calendar/isocalendar.htm
        (used with permission)
        r8
_IsoCalendarDate)
week1mondayr
isocalendarI
date.isocalendarc
ylor
date._getstatec
stringr+
__setstatek
date.__setstatec
date.__reduce__)
NNN))r
classmethodr
z}Abstract base class for time zone info classes.
    Subclasses must override the name(), utcoffset() and dst() methods.
    r
z%datetime -> string name of time zone.z&tzinfo subclass must override tzname()N
NotImplementedError
tzinfo.tznamec
zIdatetime -> timedelta, positive for east of UTC, negative for west of UTCz)tzinfo subclass must override utcoffset()Nr0
tzinfo.utcoffsetc
datetime -> DST offset as timedelta, positive for east of UTC.
        Return 0 if DST not in effect.  utcoffset() must include the DST
        offset.
        z#tzinfo subclass must override dst()Nr0
tzinfo.dstc
z*datetime in UTC -> datetime in local time.z&fromutc() requires a datetime argumentz
dt.tzinfo is not selfNz0fromutc() requires a non-None utcoffset() resultz*fromutc() requires a non-None dst() resultz;fromutc(): dt.dst gave inconsistent results; cannot convert)

, %d, %dz

, tzinfo=%r

time.__repr__rA
Return the time formatted according to ISO.
        The full format is 'HH:MM:SS.mmmmmm+zz:zz'. By default, the fractional
        part is omitted if self.microsecond == 0.
        The optional argument timespec specifies the number of additional
        terms of the time to include. Valid options are 'auto', 'hours',
        'minutes', 'seconds', 'milliseconds' and 'microseconds'.
        )
time.isoformatc
z0Construct a time from the output of isoformat().r
time_stringr
time.fromisoformatc
z{Format using strftime().  The date part of the timestamp passed
        to underlying strftime should not be used.
        il
time.strftimec
time.__format__c
z^Return the timezone offset as timedelta, positive east of UTC
         (negative west of UTC).Nr^
time.utcoffsetc
Return the timezone name.
        Note that the name is 100% informational -- there's no requirement that
        it mean anything in particular. For example, "GMT", "UTC", "-500",
        "-5:00", "EDT", "US/Eastern", "America/New York" are all valid replies.
        N
time.tznamec
Return 0 if DST is not in effect, or the DST offset (as timedelta
        positive eastward) if DST is in effect.
        This is purely informational; the DST offset has already been added to
        the UTC offset returned by utcoffset() if applicable, so there's no
        need to consult dst() unless you're interested in displaying the DST
        info.
        Nr
time.dstTc
z;Return a new time with new values for the specified fields.NTrH

protocol

Z3dbdc
datetime(year, month, day[, hour[, minute[, second[, microsecond[,tzinfo]]]]])
    The year, month and day arguments are required. tzinfo may be None, or an
    instance of a tzinfo subclass. The remaining arguments may be ints.
    Nr

Nz)tz argument must be an instance of tzinfo
myoffsetr
astimezonec
datetime.astimezonec
%s %s %2d %02d:%02d:%02d %04d)	r
datetime.ctime
Return the time formatted according to ISO.
        The full format looks like 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.mmmmmm'.
        By default, the fractional part is omitted if self.microsecond == 0.
        If self.tzinfo is not None, the UTC offset is also attached, giving
        giving a full format of 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.mmmmmm+HH:MM'.
        Optional argument sep specifies the separator between date and
        time, default 'T'.
        The optional argument timespec specifies the number of additional
        terms of the time to include. Valid options are 'auto', 'hours',
        'minutes', 'seconds', 'milliseconds' and 'microseconds'.
        z
%04d-%02d-%02d%c)
seprL
datetime.isoformatc
)	ra
mapr
datetime.__repr__c
Convert to string, for str().
datetime.__str__c
zKstring, format -> new datetime parsed from a string (like time.strptime()).r	
	_strptimeZ

        UTC).Nr^

timezone.__getinitargs__c

timezone.__hash__c
Convert to formal string, for repr().
        >>> tz = timezone.utc
        >>> repr(tz)
        'datetime.timezone.utc'
        >>> tz = timezone(timedelta(hours=-5), 'EST')
        >>> repr(tz)
        "datetime.timezone(datetime.timedelta(-1, 68400), 'EST')"
        z
datetime.timezone.utcNz	%s.%s(%r)z

<module>
